worth_it_explanation: ads.txt is the file buyers read to decide whether to trust a bid, so an incomplete or wrong file translates directly into demand the publisher never sees. The check is quick. The file is public at the domain root, and Ad Manager lists the entries it expects under Ads.txt management, so the audit is a comparison between two visible surfaces. Fixing a missing or stale entry restores eligibility for buyers that enforce ads.txt, often the same buyers paying the highest rates.
intro: Authorized Digital Sellers (ads.txt) is a public text file at a domain's root that lists the accounts a publisher authorizes to sell its inventory. Buyers fetch the file and refuse bids from sellers it does not name, which is how the file blocks counterfeit and spoofed inventory. These checks confirm the file exists, lists every authorized seller including the lines Google Ad Manager provides, carries no stale or borrowed entries, and labels each seller's relationship correctly.

audit_checks.title: The ads.txt file is missing or does not list all authorized sellers
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Buyers that enforce ads.txt will not bid on inventory whose file is missing or omits the seller account the bid comes through, so demand drops at the source. When the Google Ad Manager (GAM) lines are absent, programmatic demand routed through Google is the demand most likely to be cut.

educate.base:
Authorized Digital Sellers (ads.txt) is a plain text file a publisher hosts at the root of its domain, for example at example.com/ads.txt. Each line names a seller the publisher authorizes to sell its inventory, identified by the seller's domain, the publisher's account identifier with that seller, and whether the relationship is DIRECT or RESELLER. Buyers crawl the file and bid only on inventory that maps to a listed account, which is how ads.txt blocks counterfeit inventory that has been spoofed to look like it came from this domain.

When the file is missing, a buyer that enforces ads.txt has no way to confirm the inventory is genuine, so it declines to bid. The publisher does not see an error. The demand simply never arrives, and the inventory clears at a lower price or goes unsold. The same happens when the file exists but omits an authorized seller. A bid routed through an account the file does not name is treated as unauthorized.

Google Ad Manager generates the lines a publisher needs for inventory it sells through Google, and it surfaces them under Ads.txt management once the network has a linked Ad Exchange account. The publisher's job is to take those generated entries, add the lines for every other exchange and reseller it works with, and publish the complete list at the domain root. A file that carries some partners but drops the GAM lines, or drops a header-bidding partner, is incomplete in the way that costs the most.

A few signs point to a missing or incomplete file:
  • A request for the domain's /ads.txt returns a 404 or an empty file
  • The file is present but omits the entries GAM lists under Ads.txt management
  • A known demand partner, such as a header-bidding exchange, has no line in the file
You may want to compare the live file against the GAM-provided entries and against the publisher's current partner list before deciding the file is complete. A file that looks populated can still be missing the one account that carries the most demand.

For the format and field rules, the IAB Tech Lab ads.txt specification is the authoritative source, and Google's guide to creating ads.txt in Ad Manager covers generating and publishing the Google entries.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site that monetizes through several exchanges and a header-bidding stack, the file has to name every one of those sellers, not only Google. You may want to build the list from the GAM-provided entries plus a current inventory of header-bidding and direct partners, then confirm the published file at the domain root matches it line for line.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site that runs ads alongside its catalog, ad monetization is often a secondary revenue line, so the ads.txt file can lag behind partner changes. You may want to confirm the file exists at the root of the same domain that serves the ad inventory, rather than a subdomain or the storefront's separate domain. Buyers fetch it from the exact host that ran the ad.

investigate.intro: Fetch the domain's /ads.txt file and compare it against the entries Google Ad Manager lists under Ads.txt management, checking that the file exists and names every authorized seller.

ads_txt_file.label: Live ads.txt file
steps: Open the domain's ads.txt directly in a browser, at the root of the host that serves the ad inventory, for example the file at example.com/ads.txt.
Confirm the file loads as plain text and is not a 404, a redirect to the home page, or an empty response.
Read the entries and note which seller domains and account identifiers are present, recording the full list so you can compare it against the GAM-provided lines.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: Sign in to Google Ad Manager, open 'Admin', then 'Ads.txt management'.
Read the entries Ad Manager lists for the publisher's Google-sold inventory, and note each line it expects to see in the published file.
Compare the GAM-listed entries against the live file from the previous tool. Record any GAM line that is missing from the published file, and any current demand partner that has no line at all.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether the live /ads.txt exists and, if it does, which authorized sellers it lists against the entries Google Ad Manager provides and the publisher's current partner list. A file that exists and names every authorized seller, including the GAM-provided lines, passes. A missing file, or a file that omits the GAM entries or a current demand partner, is a fail recorded with the specific missing entries.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether the ads.txt file exists and which authorized sellers it lists, compared against the GAM-provided entries, above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s ads.txt file is missing or does not list every authorized seller, including the entries Google Ad Manager provides. Buyers that enforce Authorized Digital Sellers (ads.txt) decline to bid on inventory whose file does not name the selling account, so the gap shows up as demand that never arrives rather than as a visible error. {{voice_recommend}} generating the entries Ad Manager lists under Ads.txt management, adding the lines for every other exchange and reseller {{site_name}} works with, and publishing the complete file at the domain root. After the change, refetch the live file and confirm it names each authorized seller.

audit_checks.title: ads.txt carries stale or unauthorized seller entries
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A line for an exchange the publisher no longer works with, or a line copied from another site, leaves authorization open to a seller that should not be reselling the inventory. That is the unauthorized reselling Authorized Digital Sellers (ads.txt) exists to stop, and it dilutes the file's value as a trust signal.

educate.base:
An ads.txt file is only as trustworthy as its entries. Each line authorizes a specific seller account to sell the publisher's inventory, and that authorization stays in force until the line is removed. Lines accumulate as a publisher adds partners, and they are easy to leave behind when a partnership ends or when entries are copied from a template or another property.

A stale entry is a line for an exchange or reseller the publisher no longer works with. While it remains, that seller's account is still declared as authorized, so a bid routed through it reads as legitimate to buyers even though the relationship is over. A borrowed entry, copied from another site's file, can authorize an account that was never meant to sell this domain's inventory. Both cases widen the set of accounts buyers will trust beyond the set the publisher actually controls, which is the opposite of what the file is for.

The fix is to reconcile the file against the current partner list. Every line should map to a seller the publisher works with today, and every account identifier should be the one that seller assigned to this publisher. Google Ad Manager's syntax validation flags duplicate and malformed lines, and reviewing the authorization status of the publisher's seller accounts shows which declared accounts are still recognized.

A few signs point to stale or unauthorized entries:
  • A line names an exchange the publisher stopped working with
  • An account identifier does not match any account the publisher holds with that seller
  • The same entries appear verbatim in another site's ads.txt, suggesting a copied file
You may want to walk each line against the publisher's current partner list rather than assuming a populated file is a correct one.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site that has changed monetization partners over several years, the file often carries lines from exchanges that are no longer in the stack. You may want to date the file against the current partner list and remove any seller the publisher no longer transacts with, so the file authorizes only the accounts in use today.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site whose ads.txt was first set up by a previous agency or template, the file can carry entries that were never specific to this domain. You may want to confirm each account identifier maps to an account the publisher actually holds, rather than a value inherited from a starter file.

learn_more.source_title: Review the authorization status of your domains

investigate.intro: Review each line of the live ads.txt against the publisher's current demand partners, confirming every entry maps to a seller in use and that DIRECT versus RESELLER reflects the real account.

ads_txt_file.label: Live ads.txt file
steps: Open the domain's ads.txt and list every entry, recording the seller domain and account identifier on each line.
For each line, check the seller domain against the publisher's current list of exchanges and resellers. Flag any line for a partner the publisher no longer works with.
Look for entries that appear copied from another property, such as account identifiers the publisher does not recognize, and flag them for confirmation.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: In Ad Manager, open 'Admin', then 'Ads.txt management', and run the syntax validation to surface duplicate or malformed lines.
Review the authorization status of the publisher's seller accounts and note any declared account that is not recognized.
Cross-reference the flagged lines from the live file against this list to confirm which entries are stale or unauthorized.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per ads.txt line, whether the seller is a current partner and whether the account identifier matches an account the publisher holds. A file in which every entry maps to a current, authorized seller passes. A file carrying a line for a former partner, an unrecognized account identifier, or entries copied from another site is a fail recorded with the specific lines to remove.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record, for each ads.txt line, whether the seller is a current partner and whether the account identifier is recognized, above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s ads.txt carries stale or unauthorized seller entries, meaning lines for partners {{site_name}} no longer works with or account identifiers it does not recognize. Each such line keeps a seller declared as authorized, which is the unauthorized reselling that Authorized Digital Sellers (ads.txt) is meant to prevent, and it weakens the file as a trust signal. {{voice_recommend}} reconciling the file against the current partner list, removing any seller no longer in use, and confirming each remaining account identifier with the seller. After the change, revalidate the file in Ad Manager and confirm every entry maps to a current, authorized account.

audit_checks.title: ads.txt entries mislabel DIRECT and RESELLER relationships
audit_checks.impact_rationale: The relationship field declares whether the publisher contracts with the seller directly or the seller resells the inventory, so labeling a reseller as DIRECT misrepresents the supply path. Buyers read that field to judge how close the seller sits to the inventory, and a wrong label can reduce the trust the file is meant to build.

educate.base:
Every line in an ads.txt file ends with a relationship field that takes one of two values, DIRECT or RESELLER. DIRECT means the publisher has a direct contract with the named seller and controls the account. RESELLER means the publisher authorizes that seller to resell the inventory through an account the seller controls. The field tells a buyer how close the seller sits to the source of the inventory.

Mislabeling the field misrepresents the supply path. Marking a reseller as DIRECT implies a contractual relationship that does not exist, which can mislead buyers that weight DIRECT inventory more heavily and reduce the trust the file is built to create. Marking a direct seller as RESELLER understates the relationship and can route the inventory as if it passed through an extra hop. Either way, the declared path no longer matches the real one.

The correct value follows the contract. For inventory a publisher sells through its own Google Ad Manager account, the Google line is DIRECT. For an exchange that resells the inventory under its own seller account, the line is RESELLER. The relationship field and the structure of each line are defined in the IAB Tech Lab ads.txt specification, and Ad Manager's ads.txt documentation describes how its generated entries map to these relationships.

You may want to read the relationship field on each line against the actual contract rather than assuming the inherited value is right. The field is the last one on the line, so a copied or hand-edited entry can carry a value that no longer fits the account.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site that sells some inventory directly and resells the rest through exchanges, both DIRECT and RESELLER lines belong in the file, and the value has to match each account. You may want to confirm the lines for owned-and-operated demand read DIRECT and the exchange-resold lines read RESELLER, rather than a single value applied across the whole file.

investigate.intro: Check the DIRECT or RESELLER value on each ads.txt line against the publisher's actual relationship with that seller.

ads_txt_file.label: Live ads.txt file
steps: Open the domain's ads.txt and read the relationship field, the last value on each line, for every entry.
For each line, determine whether the publisher holds a direct contract with the seller or the seller resells the inventory, and compare that to the declared DIRECT or RESELLER value.
Flag any line where the relationship field does not match the real contract, recording the seller and the correct value.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: In Ad Manager, open 'Admin', then 'Ads.txt management', and review the relationship Ad Manager assigns to its generated Google entries.
Confirm the Google lines in the live file carry the relationship Ad Manager expects, and use the publisher's partner contracts to confirm the relationship on the remaining lines.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per ads.txt line, the declared relationship value and the real relationship with that seller. A file in which every line's relationship field matches the contract passes. A line that marks a reseller as DIRECT, or a direct seller as RESELLER, is a fail recorded with the seller and the correct value.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record, for each ads.txt line, the declared DIRECT or RESELLER value and the real relationship with the seller, above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that some of {{site_name}}'s ads.txt entries mislabel the DIRECT and RESELLER relationship, declaring a supply path that does not match the real contract. Buyers read this field to judge how close a seller sits to the inventory, so a reseller marked DIRECT, or a direct seller marked RESELLER, misrepresents the path and can reduce the trust the file is meant to build. {{voice_recommend}} setting each line's relationship field to match the actual account, with owned Google Ad Manager inventory marked DIRECT and exchange-resold inventory marked RESELLER. After the change, reread the file and confirm every relationship value matches its contract.
